standard_description: Demonstrate understanding of figurative language, word relationships, and nuances in word meanings.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Common expressions: easy
khan_exercises.description: Can you identify which prepositions to use for these common expressions?

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Some people think it’s bad luck to step on cracks in the sidewalk, but I do not believe that \_\_\_\_\_\_ all.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Not \_\_\_\_ all" is a common expression that means *not one bit.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Not at all_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Please keep your hands and feet inside the car \_\_\_\_\_\_  all times!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ all times" is a common expression that means *always.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_At all times_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  Sure, I ate your last piece of pizza, but \_\_\_\_\_\_ least I didn’t eat more than one slice!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ least" is a common expression.

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_At least_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> The ocean was tranquil until, all \_\_\_\_\_\_ once, a giant wave broke.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"All \_\_\_\_ once" is a common expression that means *suddenly.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_All at once_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  \_\_\_\_\_\_ best, this meeting will end in two hours. But I expect it’s going to take longer.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ best" is a common expression that means *best case scenario.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_At best_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Don’t eat those robin’s eggs! You’ll be  \_\_\_\_\_\_ risk of angering all the birds in the tree.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ risk" is a common expression that means *in danger.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_At risk_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  Aziz’s announcement that he was moving to Siberia took us all \_\_\_\_\_\_ surprise.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Taken \_\_\_\_ surprise" is a common expression.

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Taken by surprise_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **by**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> That’s a nice sweater! \_\_\_\_\_\_ the way, that reminds me: I lost my jacket. Have you seen it?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the way" is a common expression that means *also.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_By the way_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **by**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> The spring is a great time to visit Washington, D.C. because the weather is cool. \_\_\_\_\_\_ addition, the cherry blossoms are in bloom.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ addition" is a common expression that means *also.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_In addition_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **in**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Please order your holiday meals \_\_\_\_\_\_ advance so we have plenty of time to make them. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ advance" is a common expression that means *beforehand.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_In advance_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **in**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  \_\_\_\_\_\_ a nutshell, this movie is about a horse who fights crime.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ a nutshell" is a common expression that means *in summary.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_In a nutshell_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **in**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Coral and I both love spaghetti, mystery novels, and redwood forests; we sure have a lot \_\_\_\_\_\_ common!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"A lot \_\_\_\_ common" is a common expression that means *similar.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_A lot in common_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **in**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> When Mrs. Wong had to leave the classroom, she put Eleisha \_\_\_\_\_\_ charge.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ charge" is a common expression that means *in control.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_In charge_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **in**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Someone should tell Guillermo his shirt is on \_\_\_\_\_\_ out. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ out" is a common expression.

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Inside out_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **inside**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> I wasn’t going to buy these boots, but since they’re \_\_\_\_\_\_ sale, I can’t pass up the opportunity.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ sale" is a common expression that means *discounted.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_On sale_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **on**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Don’t leave without me; I’m \_\_\_\_\_\_ my way!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ my way" is a common expression.

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_On my way_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **on**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Dan’s Family Band packed their bags–they were going \_\_\_\_\_\_ the road!


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the road" is a common expression that means *traveling.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_On the road_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **on**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> It’s important to back \_\_\_\_\_\_, otherwise the tiger will get angry.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Back \_\_\_\_" is a common expression that means *retreat.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Back off_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **off**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Miguel liked to show \_\_\_\_\_\_ his dance moves at recess.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Show \_\_\_\_" is a common expression that means *exhibit.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Show off_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **off**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> I was dismayed to see that my favorite hardware store was now \_\_\_\_\_\_ of business.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ of business" is a common expression that means *permanently closed.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Out of business_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **out**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  Ruth didn’t notice anything \_\_\_\_\_\_ of the ordinary in the living room until a shape in the fireplace flapped its wings. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ of the ordinary" is a common expression that means *strange.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Out of the ordinary_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **out**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> You’re \_\_\_\_\_\_ of your mind if you think I’m going to let you borrow my brand-new bike.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ of your mind" is a common expression that means *crazy.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Out of your mind_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **out**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Wearing a tuxedo at the beach made Sean feel \_\_\_\_\_\_ of place.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ of place" is a common expression that means something *doesn't belong.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Out of place_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **out**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Debra tried to beat her opponent at ping pong, but she hadn’t played in two years and was \_\_\_\_\_\_ of practice.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ of practice" is a common expression that means *rusty.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Out  of practice*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **out**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> I’m sorry to postpone our water park trip, but I’m feeling \_\_\_\_\_\_ the weather. Can we go next week when I’m better?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the weather" is a common expression that means *sick.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Under the weather_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **under**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  Alanna vowed to find the person who had eaten her last piece of pizza \_\_\_\_\_\_ all costs.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ all costs" is a common expression that means *no matter what.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_At all costs_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Thank you for your hospitality and for making me feel so \_\_\_\_\_\_ home here!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ home" is a common expression that means *comfortable.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_At home_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> \_\_\_\_\_\_ long last, I am done studying and can go ice skating!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ long last" is a common expression that means *finally.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_At long last_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Bryce and Benicio argued \_\_\_\_\_\_ length about whether they should go mountain climbing or bike riding.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ length" is a common expression that means *for a long time.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_At length_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Jeannette and her sister bumped into each other by accident—neither one of them was \_\_\_\_\_\_ fault.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ fault" is a common expression that means *to blame.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_At fault_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **at**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> The hare wasn’t able to beat the tortoise—not \_\_\_\_\_\_ a long shot.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Not \_\_\_\_ a long shot" is a common expression that means *not close.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Not by a long shot_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **by**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> How funny that we should run into each other in Alaska, just \_\_\_\_\_\_ chance!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ chance" is a common expression that means *not on purpose.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_By chance_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **by**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Little Red Riding Hood thought she spotted a wolf out of the corner of her eye, but \_\_\_\_\_\_ a flash, he was gone.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ a flash" is a common expression that means *suddenly.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_In a flash_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **in**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Mr. Malouf gives annually to charity because he wants his employees to see him \_\_\_\_\_\_ a good light. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ a good light" is a common expression that means *positively.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_In a good light_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **in**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Since I haven’t gone to cooking school yet, my dream of becoming a world-famous chef is currently \_\_\_\_\_\_ of reach.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ of reach" is a common expression that means *not possible.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_Out of reach_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **out**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> When I asked Cindy if she would watch my pet lizard while I was on vacation, she smiled and said, “\_\_\_\_\_\_ pleasure!”

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ pleasure" is a common expression that means *happily.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_With pleasure_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **with**.

khan_exercises.title: Common expressions: medium
khan_exercises.description: Can you spot the correct prepositions to use in these conventional expressions?

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  This painting of a unicorn is a work \_\_\_\_\_\_ progress, but I like it so far.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ progress" is a common expression that means *unfinished.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_In progress_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **in**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> I’m so happy that strawberries are \_\_\_\_\_\_ season and I can eat as many of them as I want.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ season" is a common expression that means *ripe and at their best.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_In season  is the common expression in English, so we should use **in**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> \_\_\_\_\_\_ general, it’s not a good idea to run with scissors.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ general" is a common expression that means *usually.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_In general_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **in**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Spenser won’t slow down in this race! He’s \_\_\_\_\_\_ a roll! 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ a roll" is a common expression that means *doing well.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_On a roll_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **on**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> We made the long journey through the desert \_\_\_\_\_\_  foot.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ foot" is a common expression that means *by walking.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_On foot_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **on**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> You forgot to clean the gutters and mow the lawn? You are \_\_\_\_\_\_  thin ice, mister!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ thin ice" is a common expression that means *at risk.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_On thin ice_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **on**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> After suffering through a terrible flu for two weeks, Richard was finally \_\_\_\_\_\_ the mend.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the mend" is a common expression that means *healing.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_On the mend_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **on**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  Harold offered me the job, and I took it \_\_\_\_\_\_ the spot.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the spot" is a common expression that means *immediately.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_On the spot_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **on**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> At first I was going to say my favorite tropical rainforest animal is the toucan, but \_\_\_\_\_\_ second thought, my favorite is the tapir. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ second thought" is a common expression that means *actually.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
_On second thought_  is the common expression in English, so we should use **on**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  I know you wanted to eat that entire pecan pie, but trust me–you’re better \_\_\_\_\_\_ without it!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Better \_\_\_\_" is a common expression.

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Better off*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **off**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> The Geckos and the Bulls had to call \_\_\_\_\_\_ their football game due to bad weather.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Call \_\_\_\_" is a common expression that means *cancel.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Call off*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **off**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Today, we’re going to pick up where we left \_\_\_\_\_\_ and start reading Chapter 3 of Robinson Crusoe.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Left \_\_\_\_" is a common expression that means *stopped.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Left off*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **off**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> \_\_\_\_\_\_ the top of my head, I can name 30 countries.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the top of my head" is a common expression that means *without thinking too hard.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Off the top of my head*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **off**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> To kick \_\_\_\_\_\_ the festivities, we’re going to have a karaoke contest!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ off" is a common expression that means *start.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Kick off*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **off**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> I tend to feel out \_\_\_\_\_\_ sorts when I don’t have cereal for breakfast; cereal is my favorite way to start the day. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Out \_\_\_\_ sorts" is a common expression that means *unwell.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Out of sorts*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **of**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Mom was thrilled when her baby decided to go to sleep \_\_\_\_\_\_ his own accord.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ their own accord" is a common expression that means *on their own.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Of their own accord*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **of**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> His dad tried to wake him from a 12-hour slumber, but Hector was still \_\_\_\_\_\_ like a light.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ like a light" is a common expression that means *deep asleep.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Out like a light*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **out**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> The bat came flying and screeching over Marjorie’s shoulder, seemingly from \_\_\_\_\_\_ of nowhere.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ of nowhere" is a common expression that means *unexpectedly.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Out of nowhere*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **out**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Sorry I’m so \_\_\_\_\_\_ of it; I didn’t get enough sleep and I’m having trouble concentrating today!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ of it" is a common expression that means *dazed.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Out of it*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **out**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  “All right,” Peter said to his doctor as she prepared the needle. “Let’s get this over \_\_\_\_\_\_.” 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Over \_\_\_\_" is a common expression that means *finished.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Over with*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **with**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> \_\_\_\_\_\_ warning, the tiger leapt out of the bush and into the road.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ warning" is a common expression that means *unexpectedly.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Without warning*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **without**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> With just two weeks left until final exams, Sasha’s summer vacation was \_\_\_\_\_\_ reach.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ reach" is a common expression that means *near.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Within reach*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **within**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Since you didn’t laugh at my joke, I’m pretty sure it went \_\_\_\_\_\_ your head.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ your head" is a common expression that means someone *didn't understand.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Over your head*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **over**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> That movie was mostly good, but I thought the lead actor’s performance was a little \_\_\_\_\_\_ the top and melodramatic.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the top" is a common expression that means *exaggerated.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Over the top*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **over**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Now that Elsa was turning 20, she complained that she was getting old, but her friends insisted that she wasn’t \_\_\_\_\_\_ the hill yet.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the hill" is a common expression that means *too old.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Over the hill*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **over**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Taryn was \_\_\_\_\_\_ the moon about getting promoted to head of sales at her company.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the moon" is a common expression that means *excited.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Over the moon*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **over**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Ainsley keeps his journal \_\_\_\_\_\_ lock and key so that none of his nosy family members can read it.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ lock and key" is a common expression that means *secret* or *guarded*.

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Under lock and key*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **under**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  “Once you get a few months of working here \_\_\_\_\_\_ your belt, it’ll get easier,” Mr. Danvers said.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the belt" is a common expression that means *experienced.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
*Under the belt*  is the common expression in English, so we should use **under**.

khan_exercises.title: Common expressions: hard
khan_exercises.description: Can you spot the correct prepositions to use in these conventional expressions?

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> At his surprise party, Griffin was \_\_\_\_\_\_ a loss for words.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ a loss for words" is a common expression that means someone *doesn't know what to say.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__at__ a loss for words".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> I’m keeping myself \_\_\_\_\_\_  arm’s length from all my friends today because I have a cold.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ arm's length" is a common expression that means someone *stays far way.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__at__ arm's length".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Anthony has improved \_\_\_\_\_\_ leaps and bounds in his writing class this semester.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ leaps and bounds" is a common expression that means something *is moving quickly.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__by__ leaps and bounds".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> The soccer team’s star player was having an off night; he kept missing the goal \_\_\_\_\_\_ a mile.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Miss \_\_\_\_ a mile" is a common expression that means *to miss by a lot.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "miss __by__ a mile".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> By trying bold new recipes as well as incorporating old favorites, Chef Vince has changed food at the Chateau Cafe \_\_\_\_\_\_ a major way.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To change "\_\_\_\_ a major way" is a common expression that means to change *a lot.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__in__ a major way".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> At first, Monica was concerned when she thought she’d be expected to take care of the family’s new puppy; however, her sister’s desire to be the little bulldog’s favorite put her mind \_\_\_\_\_\_ ease.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Put one's mind \_\_\_\_ ease" is a common expression that means someone can *stop worrying.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__at__ ease".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Police say the thief who stole the million-dollar painting is still \_\_\_\_\_\_ large; they are offering a reward to anyone who might have information as to his whereabouts.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ large" is a common expression that means a criminal *hasn't been captured.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__at__ large".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  I’d love to be able to tell you where I got these magic beans, but unfortunately, I’m not \_\_\_\_\_\_ liberty to say.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Not \_\_\_\_ liberty to say" is a common expression that means I *can't tell you.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "not __at__ liberty to say".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Several weeks ago, Minerva created the perfect peanut butter and jelly sandwich. But she no longer remembered just exactly how she had crafted it; she was \_\_\_\_\_\_ her wit’s end trying to figure it out.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ her wit's end" is a common expression that means someone *is frustrated and doesn't know what to do.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__at__ her wit's end".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> My desire to go to the pumpkin patch is \_\_\_\_\_\_ odds with my wish to stay home and avoid traffic.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ odds" is a common expression that means two things are *in disagreement.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__at__ odds".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> You only have two items to buy, so \_\_\_\_\_\_ all means, please go ahead of me in line!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ all means" is a common expression that means "of course!"

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__by__ all means".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Destiny was, \_\_\_\_\_\_ all accounts, the best data scientist at the lab.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ all accounts" is a common expression that means *based on all available information.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__by__ all accounts".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> We’ve had some setbacks, but \_\_\_\_\_\_ and large, the company is seeing impressive growth this year. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ and large" is a common expression that means *for the most part.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__by__ and large".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Ms. Petersen was both admired and feared by her students because she always did things \_\_\_\_\_\_ the book and didn’t make exceptions.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the book" is a common expression that means something is done *according to all rules and regulations.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__by__ the book".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Christina won the sledding competition \_\_\_\_\_\_ virtue of the fact that she was the only participant.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ virtue of" is a common expression that means *because of.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__by__ virtue of".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Federico’s Pizza Palace has a new pizza topping \_\_\_\_\_\_ the works; I can’t wait until they release it!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the works" is a common expression that means something *is being developed.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__in__ the works".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> While I love s’mores, chocolate is also good \_\_\_\_\_\_ and of itself.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ and of itself" is a common expression that means *on its own.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__in__ and of itself".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> I could listen to this lecture about the solar system for hours \_\_\_\_\_\_  end and never get tired of it!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"For hours \_\_\_\_ end" is a common expression that means something *takes a long time.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "for hours __on__ end".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Over the years, we have stayed \_\_\_\_\_\_ good terms with our competitors, the sofa company across the street.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ good terms" is a common expression that means people *get along well.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__on__ good terms".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  \_\_\_\_\_\_  occasion, Beverly likes to walk to the duck pond and sit there for the whole afternoon, but she usually doesn’t have the time.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ occasion" is a common expression that means *once in awhile.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__on__ occasion".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> We were planning to stay home all day, but on the spur \_\_\_\_\_\_ the moment, decided to go for a five-hour drive to the beach instead!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"on the spur \_\_\_\_ the moment" is a common expression that means something is done *impulsively and suddenly*.

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "on the spur __of__ the moment".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Penny bit \_\_\_\_\_\_ more than she could chew when she volunteered to take over the librarian’s duties for a week plus keep her regular pet-sitting job.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Biting \_\_\_\_ more than you can chew" is a common expression that means someone is *trying to do more than they actually can.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "biting __off__ more than you can chew".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Juliette was upset that her family had polished \_\_\_\_\_\_ the rest of the turkey leftovers without her.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To "polish \_\_\_\_ " is a common expression that means *to finish.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "polish __off__".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> If you don’t want that box of chocolates, I’d be happy to take it \_\_\_\_\_\_ your hands!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To "take something \_\_\_\_ your hands" is a common expression that means *I'll handle it for you.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "to take something __off__ your hands".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Liesel caught Reggie  \_\_\_\_\_\_ guard with a question about foreign policy at the debate.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Caught \_\_\_\_ guard" is a common expression that means somebody *wasn't ready.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "caught __off__ guard".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> It’s been getting colder as \_\_\_\_\_\_ late now that it’s winter.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"As \_\_\_\_ late" is a common expression that means *recently.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "as __of__ late".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Jacob was \_\_\_\_\_\_ two minds about third grade: on the one hand he liked his teacher, but on the other hand, he missed the art projects he got to do in second grade. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ two minds" is a common expression that means someone *isn't sure what they think.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__of__ two minds".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> One of the Oakdale city council’s proposals \_\_\_\_\_\_ note was a bid to expand parks and other public spaces.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ note" is a common expression that means something *is important.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__of__ note".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

>  I was only 10 minutes late; you’re blowing this way \_\_\_\_\_\_ of proportion!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To blow something "\_\_\_\_ of proportion" is a common expression that means to make something *a bigger deal than it actually is.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "blown __out__ of proportion".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> All of these pranks are really getting \_\_\_\_\_\_ of hand. Yes, my refrigerator is running–stop calling me!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ of hand" is a common expression that means something is *out of control.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__out__ of hand".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Taylor passed the spelling test \_\_\_\_\_\_ flying colors; her parents were so proud!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"To pass \_\_\_\_ flying colors" is a common expression that means someone *did very well.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "to pass __with__ flying colors".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Colleen is in a bad mood today because she’s sick, so if she snaps at you, take it \_\_\_\_\_\_ a grain of salt.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Take it \_\_\_\_ a grain of salt" is a common expression that means something *shouldn't be taken too seriously.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "take it __with__ a grain of salt".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> \_\_\_\_\_\_ fail, the man in the bright orange top hat showed up at the cafe every Sunday morning.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ fail" is a common expression that means something *always happens.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__without__ fail".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> When I said you could buy as much candy as you wanted, I meant you could buy as much candy as you wanted \_\_\_\_\_\_ reason.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ reason" is a common expression that means something *isn't too much.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__within__ reason".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Ravi was pleased that the first day of the Owensville County Fair had gone off \_\_\_\_\_\_ a hitch.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Went off \_\_\_\_ a hitch" is a common expression that means something *was successful and without difficulty.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "went off __without__ a hitch".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Did you really think you could pull the wool \_\_\_\_\_\_ my eyes? I know you’re the one who’s been using all my shampoo!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Pull the wool \_\_\_\_ someone's eyes" is a common expression that means *to fool somebody*.

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "pull the wool __over__ someone's eyes".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> The new smoothie company that opened downtown is raking in money hand \_\_\_\_\_\_ fist; I guess people really love these iced drinks.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Hand \_\_\_\_ fist" is a common expression that means something happens *very quickly.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "hand __over__ fist".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> Bernard hated going \_\_\_\_\_\_ the knife, but he reminded himself that the mobility he would gain back after his knee surgery would make it all worth it.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ the knife" is a common expression that means somebody *is having surgery.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__under__ the knife".

items.question_content:
**Identify the correct preposition to use in this expression.**

> You’re \_\_\_\_\_\_ no obligation to help me eat this cupcake, but you’re welcome to!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"\_\_\_\_ no obligation" is a common expression that means somebody *isn't required to do something.*

Have you heard that phrase used with a particular preposition?
The common expression is "__under__ no obligation".
